During the Transatlantic Slave Trade, the Igbo people of present-day Nigeria were one of the largest ethnic groups affected. Many Igbo people were forcibly taken from their homes, sold into slavery, and subjected to brutal treatment. One of the most significant acts of violence against the Igbo people was the **Calabar Slave Trade**, which took place in the 16th to 19th centuries. Enslaved Igbo people were transported to the Americas, where they were forced to work on plantations, facing harsh conditions, physical abuse, and psychological trauma.

**The Yoruba People's Experience**

The Yoruba people of present-day Nigeria and Benin were also significantly affected by the Transatlantic Slave Trade. Many Yoruba people were enslaved and transported to the Americas, where they were forced to work on plantations and in mines. One of the most notable acts of violence against the Yoruba people was the **Oyo Empire's Collapse**, which took place in the 19th century. The Oyo Empire, a powerful Yoruba kingdom, was weakened by internal conflicts and external pressures, leading to its eventual collapse. This collapse led to the enslavement and displacement of many Yoruba people.

**The Ashanti People's Experience**

The Ashanti people of present-day Ghana were also affected by the Transatlantic Slave Trade. Many Ashanti people were enslaved and transported to the Americas, where they were forced to work on plantations and in mines. One of the most significant acts of violence against the Ashanti people was the **Ashanti Wars**, which took place in the 19th century. The Ashanti Wars were a series of conflicts between the Ashanti Empire and European colonizers, resulting in the enslavement and displacement of many Ashanti people.

**The Dahomey People's Experience**

The Dahomey people of present-day Benin were also affected by the Transatlantic Slave Trade. Many Dahomey people were enslaved and transported to the Americas, where they were forced to work on plantations and in mines.
